s: P-21A collected nighttime ionosphere data to improve space communications.
q: Why did P-21A study the nighttime ionosphere?
e: The ionosphere changes at night, and P-21A flew up to find out why radio signals get weird after dark!

s: Prognoz 2 detected cosmic gamma-ray bursts and solar flare events.
q: What cosmic events did Prognoz 2 detect?
e: Soviet satellite Prognoz 2 caught mysterious gamma-ray explosions from deep space!

s: The Recycled Spacecraft is a real spacecraft monument located in Livorno, Italy, built from actual space hardware.
q: Where is the Recycled Spacecraft monument located?
e: Located in Livorno, Italy, this unique art installation and monument incorporates genuine spacecraft components. It highlights the growing importance of sustainability in space exploration and the creative reuse of space technology artifacts.

s: Relict 2 studied the universe in submillimeter wavelengths and magnetotail plasma.
q: What wavelength did Relict 2 observe the universe in?
e: Relict 2 looked at the universe in light waves shorter than a millimeter to study ancient cosmic radiation!

s: Astronauts have practiced various religions in space, including reading scripture and taking communion on the Moon.
q: Which Apollo 11 astronaut took communion on the Moon?
e: Buzz Aldrin took communion on the Moon during Apollo 11 in 1969. Muslim astronauts have prayed toward Mecca from the ISS, and a Torah scroll has been carried to space. Space agencies have guidelines for religious practice in the unique environment of spaceflight.

s: S3-3 carried eight sensors to study Earth's magnetosphere from polar orbit.
q: How many sensors did the S3-3 satellite carry?
e: This tiny satellite packed eight different instruments to study Earth's invisible magnetic bubble!

s: San Marco-D/L was Italy's atmospheric research satellite.
q: Which country built the San Marco-D/L satellite?
e: Italy launched satellites from a platform in the Indian Ocean off the coast of Kenya!

s: Shenzhou 11 carried two taikonauts to the Tiangong-2 space laboratory for a 30-day stay in 2016.
q: How long did the Shenzhou 11 crew stay aboard Tiangong-2?
e: Shenzhou 11 launched in October 2016 and docked with Tiangong-2, China's second space lab. The crew of Jing Haipeng and Chen Dong spent 30 days in orbit conducting experiments in medicine, physics, and biology.

s: Shenzhou 13 was a crewed spaceflight of China's Shenzhou program that docked with the Tiangong space station.
q: What was notable about the Shenzhou 13 mission?
a: It was China's longest crewed spaceflight at the time
d: First Chinese female taikonaut in space
e: Launched in October 2021, Shenzhou 13 carried three taikonauts to the Tiangong space station for a six-month stay. It was the longest crewed Chinese spaceflight at the time and included China's first female spacewalk by Wang Yaping.

s: Shenzhou 16 was a crewed mission to the Tiangong space station that included China's first civilian astronaut.
q: What milestone did Shenzhou 16 achieve for China's space program?
a: It carried China's first civilian astronaut
e: Launched in May 2023, Shenzhou 16 carried three crew members including Gui Haichao, a university professor who became China's first civilian in space. The mission conducted over 80 scientific experiments aboard Tiangong.

s: SMS 2 was a NASA-developed weather satellite operated by NOAA that pioneered geostationary weather observation.
q: What orbit type did SMS 2 use for weather monitoring?
e: The Synchronous Meteorological Satellite 2, launched in 1975, orbited at 36,000 km altitude where it stayed fixed over one spot on Earth. SMS 2 and its predecessor SMS 1 proved that continuous weather monitoring from geostationary orbit was feasible, leading to the GOES satellite series still used today.

s: Solar-C is a planned Japanese-led Sun-observing satellite that will study the solar atmosphere in extreme ultraviolet.
q: What wavelength of light will Solar-C primarily observe?
e: Solar-C (also called SOLAR-C EUVST) will carry an extreme ultraviolet spectroscopic telescope to study how energy flows through the Sun's atmosphere. Understanding solar dynamics helps predict space weather that can affect satellites, power grids, and communications on Earth.

s: SOLRAD 11A orbited at 20 Earth radii to monitor solar radiation.
q: How far from Earth did SOLRAD 11A orbit?
e: SOLRAD 11A orbited 20 times farther than Earth's radius to watch the Sun from way out!

s: Soyuz 7K-T was the second generation of Soyuz spacecraft, designed to ferry crews to Soviet space stations.
q: Why was the Soyuz 7K-T redesigned to carry only two crew?
a: To allow room for pressure suits after the Soyuz 11 tragedy
e: Developed after the Soyuz 11 tragedy where three cosmonauts died during re-entry, the 7K-T version was redesigned to carry only two crew members wearing pressure suits. It served as the primary crew transport from 1973 to 1981.

s: Soyuz GVK was a planned Russian reusable cargo spacecraft.
q: What made Soyuz GVK unusual for a cargo craft?
d: It was designed for cargo only
It was operated by the US
It never completed a test flight
It was smaller than regular Soyuz
e: Russia planned a cargo ship that could fly to space stations and come back to fly again!

s: Soyuz-P was a proposed Soviet combat spacecraft designed to intercept enemy satellites.
q: What was the proposed purpose of Soyuz-P?
a: To intercept and disable enemy satellites
d: To transport cosmonauts to space stations
e: In the 1960s, the Soviet Union designed Soyuz-P as a crewed anti-satellite weapon. A cosmonaut would approach an enemy satellite, inspect it, and either disable or destroy it. The concept was eventually abandoned in favor of uncrewed interceptors.

s: Soyuz-T was an upgraded Soviet crewed spacecraft that restored the three-person crew capacity.
q: What major improvement did Soyuz-T bring over Soyuz 7K-T?
e: Introduced in 1979, Soyuz-T featured a new digital flight computer, solar panels for longer missions, and improved propulsion. It restored the three-crew configuration lost after the Soyuz 11 disaster, thanks to lighter pressure suits called Sokol.

s: SPARTAN 101 was a free-flying X-ray astronomy satellite deployed and retrieved by the Space Shuttle during STS-51-G.
q: How were SPARTAN satellites recovered after observations?
e: SPARTAN satellites were small, reusable spacecraft deployed from the Shuttle's cargo bay. They would observe astronomical targets for a day or two, then the Shuttle would pick them up and bring them home for data analysis and reflight.

s: SPARTAN 201-03 was a free-flying solar observatory deployed during Space Shuttle mission STS-69.
q: What did SPARTAN 201-03 study?
a: The Sun's corona and solar wind
e: SPARTAN 201-03 studied the Sun's corona and solar wind using ultraviolet and white-light instruments. It was deployed from the Shuttle, observed the Sun for about 48 hours, then was recaptured by the Shuttle's robotic arm.

s: Spartan-C surveyed star fields and captured ultraviolet images of nebulae.
q: What type of light did Spartan-C use to image nebulae?
e: Spartan-C could see galaxies in ultraviolet light invisible to human eyes!

s: SPARTAN 201-04 was a free-flying solar observatory deployed from the Space Shuttle.
q: How was SPARTAN 201-04 deployed in space?
e: Astronauts tossed a telescope out of the shuttle, let it float free to study the Sun, then caught it again!

s: SSLV-D1 was India's first Small Satellite Launch Vehicle flight.
q: What was SSLV-D1 for India?
e: India built a mini rocket specifically designed to launch small satellites quickly and cheaply!

s: STS/STORE tested components for Gravity Probe-B to verify Einstein's relativity.
q: Whose theory did Gravity Probe-B aim to test?
e: NASA built a spacecraft specifically to prove Einstein right about how gravity warps space!

s: STSAT-2A was South Korea's first satellite launched on a domestically built rocket, though the mission failed.
q: Why did the STSAT-2A mission fail?
e: Launched in 2009 on the KSLV-1 (Naro) rocket, STSAT-2A failed to reach orbit when the payload fairing didn't separate properly. Despite the failure, it marked South Korea's entry into the space launch club and paved the way for later successes.

s: SUSIE is a proposed European vertical-landing lifting body spacecraft.
q: What type of spacecraft is Europe's SUSIE?
e: Europe is designing a spacecraft shaped like a flying wing that lands vertically like a rocket!

s: Tiantong-1 is China's first mobile telecommunications satellite, providing voice and data services.
q: What service does China's Tiantong-1 satellite provide?
e: Launched in 2016, Tiantong-1 provides satellite phone and data communications for mobile users in China and surrounding areas. It enables communication in remote regions where terrestrial networks don't reach, similar to Inmarsat and Thuraya systems.

s: Tianzhou 9 is a Chinese cargo spacecraft designed to resupply the Tiangong space station.
q: What is the primary purpose of the Tianzhou spacecraft?
e: The Tianzhou series are uncrewed cargo vehicles that deliver food, fuel, experiments, and equipment to China's Tiangong station. Each Tianzhou can carry about 6.9 tonnes of supplies to orbit.

s: TV-D1 was India's 2023 test flight for the Gaganyaan crew escape system.
q: What did India's TV-D1 mission test?
e: India's ISRO conducted the TV-D1 mission to test the crew escape system for Gaganyaan, India's first crewed spaceflight program. The test demonstrated that the escape system could pull the crew module away from the rocket in case of an emergency during launch.

s: UK-DMC was a British remote sensing satellite that was part of the Disaster Monitoring Constellation.
q: What was the purpose of the Disaster Monitoring Constellation?
e: Launched in 2003, UK-DMC could rapidly image disaster zones like floods, earthquakes, and wildfires. The Disaster Monitoring Constellation was a group of small satellites from multiple countries that shared imaging capacity to respond quickly to natural disasters worldwide.

s: Vela 6B was a spin-stabilized satellite designed to detect nuclear explosions in space.
q: What treaty did the Vela satellite program monitor?
e: The Vela program was created to monitor the 1963 Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty, which prohibited nuclear tests in space, the atmosphere, and underwater. Vela satellites accidentally discovered gamma-ray bursts from deep space, one of the most energetic phenomena in the universe.

s: Vela 5B was a polyhedral satellite designed to detect nuclear tests in space.
q: What was the Vela 5B satellite designed to detect?
e: The Vela satellites were secret nuclear watchdogs that accidentally discovered cosmic gamma-ray bursts!

s: X-15 Flight 90 was a 1963 American sub-orbital spaceflight that reached extreme altitudes for aerodynamic research.
q: How many total flights did the X-15 program conduct?
e: The X-15 program conducted 199 flights between 1959 and 1968, gathering data on hypersonic flight, re-entry heating, and high-altitude control. Flight 90 contributed to understanding how aircraft behave at the boundary between atmosphere and space.

s: X-15 Flight 91 in 1963 was an American crewed mission that achieved sub-orbital spaceflight by exceeding 80 km altitude.
q: What type of vehicle was the X-15?
e: The X-15 was a rocket-powered aircraft that pushed the boundaries of flight, reaching the edge of space. Flight 91 was one of several missions where the X-15 crossed the 80 km altitude threshold used by the US Air Force to define spaceflight.

s: Neutron stars are so dense that a teaspoon of their material would weigh about 6 billion tons on Earth.
q: Approximately how much would a teaspoon of neutron star material weigh on Earth?
e: When massive stars collapse, their protons and electrons merge into neutrons, creating matter so dense it defies everyday intuition — a sugar-cube-sized chunk outweighs Mount Everest.

s: Europa, one of Jupiter's moons, likely has more liquid water beneath its icy crust than all of Earth's oceans combined.
q: Which moon of Jupiter is believed to have more water than all of Earth's oceans?
e: Despite being smaller than our Moon, Europa's subsurface ocean may hold 2-3 times Earth's water volume, making it one of the most promising places to search for extraterrestrial life.

s: The James Webb Space Telescope orbits the Sun at a point called L2, about 1.5 million kilometers from Earth — four times farther than the Moon.
q: Where does the James Webb Space Telescope orbit?
a: At the L2 Lagrange point, 1.5 million km from Earth
e: JWST doesn't orbit Earth like Hubble. It sits at the second Lagrange point where Earth's and the Sun's gravity balance, keeping it in a stable position with its sunshield always facing the Sun.

s: The cosmic microwave background radiation is the oldest light in the universe, released about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
q: How old is the cosmic microwave background radiation?
a: About 380,000 years after the Big Bang
d: About 1 million years after the Big Bang
About 100,000 years after the Big Bang
About 1 billion years after the Big Bang
At the moment of the Big Bang
About 50,000 years after the Big Bang
About 10 million years after the Big Bang
e: Before this moment, the universe was so hot and dense that light couldn't travel freely. When it cooled enough for atoms to form, photons were released and have been traveling ever since — now detected as faint microwaves.

s: Dark energy makes up about 68% of the universe and is causing the expansion of the universe to accelerate.
q: What percentage of the universe is made up of dark energy?
e: Discovered in 1998 by observing distant supernovae, dark energy is the most abundant yet least understood component of the cosmos — it's pushing galaxies apart faster and faster.

s: On Mars, sunsets appear blue because fine dust particles in the atmosphere scatter red light away and let blue light through.
q: What color are sunsets on Mars?
e: The opposite of Earth's orange sunsets! Martian dust is the perfect size to scatter longer red wavelengths, so the sky around the setting Sun takes on an eerie blue glow.

s: Gravitational waves were first directly detected in 2015 by LIGO, confirming Einstein's 100-year-old prediction.
q: When were gravitational waves first directly detected?
e: Two merging black holes 1.3 billion light-years away created ripples in spacetime so tiny they compressed and stretched LIGO's 4km laser arms by less than a proton's width.

s: The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object — over 24 billion kilometers from Earth.
q: What is the most distant human-made object from Earth?
e: Still transmitting data after nearly 50 years, Voyager 1 entered interstellar space in 2012 and carries a Golden Record with sounds and images of Earth for any extraterrestrial finders.

s: A day on Venus is longer than its year — Venus takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.
q: What is unusual about a day on Venus compared to its year?
a: A day is longer than a year
d: Its day is shorter than its year
Its year is longer than its day
It rotates perpendicular to its orbit
Its rotation is synchronized with Earth
e: Venus rotates so slowly (and backwards compared to most planets) that the Sun would rise in the west and set in the east, and you'd celebrate a birthday before a single day passed.

s: The Perseverance rover on Mars carries a small helicopter called Ingenuity — the first aircraft to achieve powered flight on another planet.
q: What is the name of the first aircraft to fly on another planet?
e: Ingenuity was designed for just 5 flights but completed over 70, proving that powered flight is possible in Mars's thin atmosphere which is just 1% as dense as Earth's.

s: The International Space Station orbits Earth about 16 times per day, meaning astronauts see 16 sunrises and sunsets every 24 hours.
q: How many times does the ISS orbit Earth per day?
e: Traveling at 28,000 km/h at an altitude of about 400 km, the ISS completes one orbit every 90 minutes, giving crew members a constantly changing view of our planet.

s: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches — roughly 200 sextillion (2×10²³) stars.
q: Approximately how many stars are estimated to exist in the observable universe?
e: This staggering number means that for every grain of sand on Earth, there are roughly 10 stars in the universe. And each star could have its own system of planets.

s: Saturn's rings are incredibly thin — while spanning 282,000 km in diameter, they're only about 10 meters thick on average.
q: How thick are Saturn's rings on average?
e: If Saturn's rings were scaled down to the width of a football field, they would be 10,000 times thinner than a razor blade. They're mostly made of ice particles ranging from tiny grains to house-sized chunks.

s: A magnetar is a type of neutron star with a magnetic field so strong it could erase your credit card from halfway across the solar system.
q: What is a magnetar?
a: A neutron star with an extremely strong magnetic field
d: A white dwarf with intense gravity
A black hole with a corona
e: Magnetars have magnetic fields roughly a quadrillion times stronger than Earth's. At close range, these fields would distort atoms into pencil shapes and make normal matter impossible.

s: Enceladus, a moon of Saturn, shoots geysers of water ice into space from cracks near its south pole — some of this material feeds Saturn's E ring.
q: Which moon of Saturn shoots water ice geysers into space?
e: The Cassini spacecraft flew through these plumes and detected organic molecules, salt water, and silica particles — strong evidence for hydrothermal vents on Enceladus's ocean floor.

s: A pulsar is a rapidly spinning neutron star that emits beams of radiation like a cosmic lighthouse, some spinning over 700 times per second.
q: How fast can the fastest known pulsars spin?
e: The fastest known pulsar, PSR J1748-2446ad, spins 716 times per second — its surface moves at nearly 25% the speed of light, making it one of the most extreme objects in nature.

s: The Great Red Spot on Jupiter is a storm larger than Earth that has been raging for at least 350 years.
q: How long has Jupiter's Great Red Spot been observed?
e: First observed in the 1600s, this anticyclonic storm features wind speeds up to 680 km/h. It has been slowly shrinking over the past century but remains the largest storm in the solar system.
